title: The EndocrinologyPsychiatry InterfaceHow Do We Help Each Other
authors: Elizabeth J. Murphy, MD, DPhil; Kewchang Lee, MD

items.title: Why hyperprolactinemia is common on antipsychotics
items.text: Most antipsychotics act as D2 receptor antagonists; first-generation agents and risperidone/paliperidone elevate prolactin in most patients without underlying prolactinoma.
items.reference: Antipsychotic Agents and Hyperprolactinemia: "Most antipsychotic agents work in part as dopamine D_{2} receptor antagonists. Thus, it is not surprising that hyperprolactinemia is a common adverse effect."

items.title: Why dopamine agonists may worsen psychiatric disease
items.text: Treating drug-induced hyperprolactinemia with cabergoline while on dopamine antagonists is like 'stepping on gas and brake' and may exacerbate psychiatric symptoms.
items.reference: Case 1: "starting one of these medications while being treated with an agent that is acting as a dopamine antagonist is a little like stepping on the gas and the brake at the same time"

items.title: How to manage amenorrhea from paliperidone-induced hyperprolactinemia
items.text: With depot paliperidone and well-controlled schizophrenia, estrogen replacement for bone health is preferred over dopamine agonists or risky antipsychotic switches.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: C) Start an oral contraceptive pill or other estrogen replacement"

items.title: When to defer MRI in medication-induced hyperprolactinemia
items.text: On known prolactin-raising agents, prolactin <100 ng/mL often does not need MRI; risperidone/phenothiazines can exceed 200 ng/mL without tumor.
items.reference: Antipsychotic Agents and Hyperprolactinemia: "In patients on an antipsychotic agent known to increase prolactin who have a prolactin concentration less than 100 ng/mL (<4.3 nmol/L), many clinicians would not routinely recommend MRI."

items.title: How to monitor antipsychotic metabolic adverse effects
items.text: Screen glucose and lipids per consensus guidance; consider BMI and diabetes checks as early as 4–6 weeks after initiation given rapid weight and glycemic changes.
items.reference: Management of Metabolic Changes: "Given the significant patient variability and what can be very rapid-onset changes, earlier initial evaluation (eg, 4-6 weeks after start of treatment) may be most appropriate"

items.title: Why olanzapine can cause rapid severe hyperglycemia
items.text: Olanzapine causes rapid weight gain and direct effects on insulin signaling and pancreatic beta cells—DKA can occur even without major weight gain.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: C) Olanzapine"

items.title: How to manage acute antipsychotic-induced hyperglycemia
items.text: Symptomatic glucose 420 mg/dL with rapid HbA1c rise requires insulin acutely; SGLT2 inhibitors or nutrition alone are insufficient.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: D) Start insulin"

items.title: Why liothyronine is used for refractory depression
items.text: Despite limited blinded data, liothyronine augmentation remains an option for life-threatening treatment-resistant depression after failed antidepressant trials.
items.reference: Liothyronine as Augmentation Therapy for Depression: "Strategies for treatment-refractory depression include augmentation therapy with nonantidepressant medications such as liothyronine (T₃)."

items.title: How liothyronine causes low T4 with suppressed TSH
items.text: Exogenous T3 suppresses TSH and lowers T4 via pituitary-thyroid axis—Case 2 patient on 75 mcg liothyronine had T3 thyrotoxicosis pattern.
items.reference: Case 2: "further testing in the outpatient setting showed he had T_{3} thyrotoxicosis. This results in suppression of TSH and a subsequently low T_{4} level."

items.title: How to monitor liothyronine augmentation safely
items.text: Target TSH at lower limit of normal; assess bone mineral density by DXA every 2 years to limit hyperthyroid complications.
items.reference: Liothyronine as Augmentation Therapy for Depression: "It is also recommended to assess bone mineral density by DXA every 2 years while on therapy."

items.title: Why endocrine-psychiatry communication is essential
items.text: Never stop or change antipsychotics without psychiatric consultation; collaborative decisions balance psychiatric stability against endocrine harm.
items.reference: Antipsychotic Agents and Hyperprolactinemia: "stopping an antipsychotic medication can be difficult and should never be done without consultation with the patient's mental health care provider."

items.title: Why discordant TFTs in hospitalized psychiatric patients have broad differential
items.text: Low TSH with low free T4 may reflect central hypothyroidism, euthyroid sick syndrome, assay interference, or T3 thyrotoxicosis—Answer E any of the above.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: E) Any of the above"

items.question: 38-year-old on paliperidone depot, prolactin 125 ng/mL, 2-mm pituitary lesion, amenorrhea 2 years, stable psychosis. Best step?
options: Switch antipsychotic immediately without psychiatry input
items.explanation: Estrogen for hypogonadism/bone protection preferred over dopamine agonist or high-risk medication switch.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: C) Start an oral contraceptive pill or other estrogen replacement"

items.question: Hospitalized patient: TSH 0.02, free T4 0.52. Possible explanations include:
items.explanation: Broad differential until outpatient clarification—ultimately liothyronine-induced T3 thyrotoxicosis.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: E) Any of the above"

items.question: 5 weeks after olanzapine for schizophrenia relapse, weight up 11 kg, glucose 420 mg/dL, HbA1c 9.1%. Most likely cause?
items.explanation: Rapid profound weight gain and hyperglycemia classic for olanzapine metabolic toxicity.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: C) Olanzapine"

items.question: Same patient at visit with polyuria/polydipsia and glucose 420 mg/dL. Most appropriate intervention now?
items.explanation: Acute symptomatic severe hyperglycemia requires insulin; psychiatry collaboration for antipsychotic change later.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: D) Start insulin"

items.question: Which antipsychotic has minimal prolactin effect?
items.explanation: Aripiprazole has partial agonist activity and minimal prolactin elevation per table.
items.reference: Table. Effect of Common Antipsychotic Medications on Increasing Prolactin: "Aripiprazole ... -"

items.question: Highest prolactin elevation (+++) is seen with:
items.explanation: Risperidone, paliperidone, and amisulpride are +++ in the table.
items.reference: Table. Effect of Common Antipsychotic Medications on Increasing Prolactin: "Risperidone ... +++"

items.question: Prolactin 80 ng/mL on risperidone with temporal link to drug start. MRI:
options: May be deferred by many clinicians if medication clearly causal and prolactin modest
items.explanation: Medication-induced hyperprolactinemia with clear temporal relationship may not need urgent MRI if <100 ng/mL on known agent.
items.reference: Antipsychotic Agents and Hyperprolactinemia: "prolactin concentration less than 100 ng/mL (<4.3 nmol/L), many clinicians would not routinely recommend MRI."

items.question: Most weight-neutral antipsychotics with less diabetes risk include:
items.explanation: Ziprasidone and aripiprazole are considered more weight-neutral with fewer diabetes-related events.
items.reference: Management of Metabolic Changes: "Ziprasidone and aripiprazole are thought to be the most weight-neutral options with less diabetes-related adverse events"

items.question: Typical starting liothyronine augmentation dose for depression is:
options: 25 mcg daily increasing to 50 mcg after a week
items.explanation: Psychiatric literature commonly starts 25 mcg daily, titrating to 50 mcg.
items.reference: Liothyronine as Augmentation Therapy for Depression: "The dosing recommendation is typically 25 mcg daily increased to 50 mcg daily after a week"

items.question: STAR*D trial suggested liothyronine augmentation vs lithium had:
options: Slight advantages in effectiveness and tolerability vs lithium
items.explanation: STAR*D authors noted slight advantages for T3 over lithium though study had limitations.
items.reference: Liothyronine as Augmentation Therapy for Depression: "T_{3} has slight advantages over lithium in effectiveness and tolerability."

items.question: Primary clinical harm of antipsychotic hyperprolactinemia is:
options: Hypogonadism leading to amenorrhea/low testosterone and bone loss
items.explanation: Hypogonadism from prolactin suppression of GnRH is the main clinically important consequence.
items.reference: Antipsychotic Agents and Hyperprolactinemia: "the primary adverse effect of hyperprolactinemia is due to subsequent hypogonadism."

items.question: Routine prolactin monitoring on antipsychotics is:
items.explanation: Monitor for galactorrhea, amenorrhea, decreased libido rather than routine prolactin levels.
items.reference: Antipsychotic Agents and Hyperprolactinemia: "we do not recommend routinely checking prolactin levels in patients on these medications."

items.question: Safety monitoring during liothyronine augmentation should include:
options: TSH at lower limit of normal and DXA every 2 years
items.explanation: Updated guidance recommends TFT and periodic DXA during T3 augmentation.
items.reference: Liothyronine as Augmentation Therapy for Depression: "assess bone mineral density by DXA every 2 years while on therapy."

items.statement: Antipsychotic hyperprolactinemia results primarily from D2 receptor antagonism.
items.explanation: D2 blockade disinhibits prolactin secretion.
items.reference: Antipsychotic Agents and Hyperprolactinemia: "Most antipsychotic agents work in part as dopamine D_{2} receptor antagonists."

items.statement: Routine prolactin measurement is recommended for all patients on antipsychotics.
items.explanation: Monitor symptoms; routine labs not recommended.
items.reference: Antipsychotic Agents and Hyperprolactinemia: "we do not recommend routinely checking prolactin levels in patients on these medications."

items.statement: Cabergoline is the best first step for paliperidone-induced hyperprolactinemia with amenorrhea.
items.explanation: Dopamine agonist risks psychiatric destabilization; estrogen replacement preferred.
items.reference: Case 1: "treating with a dopamine agonist (Answer A) ... carries with it the potential for exacerbating her psychiatric condition."

items.statement: Olanzapine can cause >7% body weight gain rapidly.
items.explanation: Olanzapine among agents with largest weight gains, sometimes >7% baseline.
items.reference: Antipsychotic Agents and Metabolic Alterations: "olanzapine, clozapine, zotepine, and chlorpromazine result in some of the largest weight gains, which can be rapid and significant (gain of >7% of baseline weight)."

items.statement: Antipsychotic-associated DKA can occur without significant weight gain.
items.explanation: Direct beta-cell and insulin signaling effects independent of weight.
items.reference: Antipsychotic Agents and Metabolic Alterations: "acute decompensation with diabetic ketoacidosis ... in patients without weight gain."

items.statement: Liothyronine augmentation requires thyroid function and bone density monitoring.
items.explanation: Safety monitoring now recommended despite limited efficacy data.
items.reference: Liothyronine as Augmentation Therapy for Depression: "Subsequent guidelines now recommend safety monitoring."

items.statement: T3 thyrotoxicosis from liothyronine can show suppressed TSH with low free T4.
items.explanation: Exogenous T3 suppresses TSH and lowers T4.
items.reference: Case 2: "T_{3} thyrotoxicosis. This results in suppression of TSH and a subsequently low T_{4} level."

items.statement: Antipsychotic changes for endocrine adverse effects require psychiatric collaboration.
items.explanation: Never stop or switch antipsychotics without mental health provider input.
items.reference: Antipsychotic Agents and Hyperprolactinemia: "should never be done without consultation with the patient's mental health care provider."

items.assertion: Assertion: Estrogen replacement is preferred over dopamine agonist in Case 1.
items.reason: Reason: Because cabergoline always improves psychosis on concurrent antipsychotics.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—agonist may worsen psychosis.
items.reference: Case 1: "providing estrogen for bone health (Answer C) would be the best option"

items.assertion: Assertion: Prolactin <100 ng/mL on known prolactin-raising antipsychotic may not require MRI.
items.reason: Reason: Because all prolactin elevation mandates immediate surgery.
items.explanation: Assertion true per guidelines nuance; reason false.
items.reference: Antipsychotic Agents and Hyperprolactinemia: "many clinicians would not routinely recommend MRI."

items.assertion: Assertion: Olanzapine likely caused rapid worsening glycemic control in Case 3.
items.reason: Reason: Because the rapid 11-kg weight gain pattern fits olanzapine toxicity.
items.explanation: Both true—rapid weight gain and direct metabolic effects.
items.reference: Case 3: "The rapidity with which he has gained such a significant amount of weight is very suggestive of an olanzapine adverse effect"

items.assertion: Assertion: Insulin is required acutely for glucose 420 mg/dL with symptoms in Case 3.
items.reason: Reason: Because SGLT2 inhibitors alone correct DKA-level hyperglycemia immediately.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—insulin needed for acute severe hyperglycemia.
items.reference: Case 3: "he needs insulin (Answer D) acutely."

items.assertion: Assertion: Liothyronine remains an option for refractory severe depression.
items.reason: Reason: Because large blinded RCTs proved definitive IQ-level efficacy.
items.explanation: Assertion true per practice; reason overstates evidence quality.
items.reference: Liothyronine as Augmentation Therapy for Depression: "Despite limited data on its clinical efficacy, liothyronine (T₃) remains a consideration"

items.assertion: Assertion: Liothyronine therapy can produce T3 thyrotoxicosis with low T4.
items.reason: Reason: Because T3 suppresses TSH and lowers T4 concentrations.
items.explanation: Both true per Case 2 mechanism.
items.reference: Case 2: "T_{3} thyrotoxicosis. This results in suppression of TSH and a subsequently low T_{4} level."

items.assertion: Assertion: Aripiprazole has minimal prolactin elevation.
items.reason: Reason: Because it acts as a pure D2 antagonist without agonist activity.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—it has partial agonist properties.
items.reference: Table. Effect of Common Antipsychotic Medications on Increasing Prolactin: "Aripiprazole ... -"

items.assertion: Assertion: Early metabolic monitoring (4–6 weeks) may catch rapid antipsychotic adverse effects.
items.reason: Reason: Because metabolic changes only occur after 5 years of therapy.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—changes can be rapid within weeks.
items.reference: Management of Metabolic Changes: "earlier initial evaluation (eg, 4-6 weeks after start of treatment) may be most appropriate"
